worth_it_explanation: New-to-brand (NTB) metrics separate first-time brand purchasers from repeat buyers, which is the signal that tells you whether prospecting is doing its job. Without it, a campaign that drives only repeat buyers can look like a success on total purchases while contributing nothing to customer acquisition. Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC) lets you cross-tab NTB rates against audience segments, line items, and creative, so the check is practical to act on once the metric is visible. The fix is a reporting and goal-setting change, not a rebuild.
intro: New-to-brand metrics in Amazon DSP track whether a purchase came from a customer who had not bought from the brand on Amazon in the prior 12 months. They exist specifically to measure customer acquisition separate from repeat purchase. These checks confirm that NTB metrics are being read, that campaign goals reference NTB where acquisition is the objective, and that NTB and retargeting data are not being combined in a way that inflates the acquisition story.

audit_checks.title: New-to-brand metrics are not being reviewed alongside total purchases
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A prospecting campaign measured only on total purchases cannot distinguish customer acquisition from repeat purchases. The campaign may be generating revenue while contributing nothing to brand growth, and no one would see it in the data.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP reports new-to-brand (NTB) metrics at the order and line item level. An NTB purchase is one where the buyer had not purchased from the brand on Amazon in the prior 12 months. The metric separates first-time brand purchasers from repeat buyers so prospecting campaigns can be evaluated on the outcome they are designed to drive: new customers.

Total purchases and NTB purchases tell different stories. A line item that drives a high volume of total purchases but a low NTB rate is reaching existing customers, not growing the brand. A line item with a lower total purchase volume but a high NTB rate is actually acquiring new buyers. Measuring only total purchases collapses that distinction and makes it impossible to see which campaigns are working for acquisition.

Amazon DSP surfaces NTB metrics in the console at the order and line item level. Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC) supports deeper analysis, including cross-tabbing NTB rate against audience segments, creative, and frequency. You may want to confirm that the reporting view includes NTB purchases and NTB rate alongside total purchases, so the two can be compared directly.

A few signs point to NTB metrics not being reviewed:
  • Reporting views show only total purchases with no NTB column visible
  • Post-campaign analysis references only return on ad spend (ROAS) or total sales without a separate NTB line
  • Prospecting and retargeting line items are evaluated on the same purchase metric with no NTB breakout
You may want to add NTB purchases and NTB rate to the standard reporting view for any campaign that includes a prospecting objective.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers selling products on Amazon, NTB rate is one of the primary signals for prospecting efficiency. A product detail page view or add-to-cart from a net-new buyer converts into an NTB purchase, and the NTB rate on a prospecting line item tells you whether that funnel is working for customer acquisition rather than just driving repeat purchases from existing buyers.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Non-endemic advertisers driving to off-Amazon destinations do not have access to Amazon's NTB purchase metrics because those metrics depend on Amazon's order data. Non-endemic campaigns are measured through the Amazon Ad Tag pixel and focus on site events rather than purchases. This check applies to endemic advertisers only.

investigate.intro: Confirm that NTB purchases and NTB rate are visible in reporting and that they are being reviewed separately from total purchases for prospecting line items.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and navigate to the order or line item you want to review.
In the reporting view, check whether NTB purchases and NTB rate are included as columns. If they are not visible, use the column selector to add them.
Compare total purchases to NTB purchases for each prospecting line item. Note the NTB rate as a percentage of total purchases.
Identify any prospecting line items where NTB purchases are not broken out or where NTB rate has not been reviewed in recent performance summaries.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: In AMC, run a query that breaks out NTB purchases by line item or audience segment to see whether acquisition rate varies across targeting approaches.
If NTB data has not been queried at all, note that as a gap. AMC allows NTB analysis at a level of granularity the console alone cannot provide.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner confirms, per prospecting order or line item, whether NTB purchases and NTB rate are visible in the reporting view and whether they have been reviewed in recent performance analysis. A line item with NTB metrics visible and referenced in performance review passes. A line item where NTB metrics are absent from the reporting view or have not been reviewed is a fail recorded with the order or line item name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the NTB reporting status for each prospecting order or line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that NTB purchases and NTB rate are not being reviewed alongside total purchases for {{site_name}}'s prospecting campaigns in Amazon DSP. Without that breakout, there is no way to confirm whether prospecting is acquiring new customers or reaching buyers who would have purchased regardless. {{voice_recommend}} adding NTB purchases and NTB rate columns to the standard reporting view for all prospecting orders and line items, and reviewing them as the primary acquisition metrics for any campaign with a customer-growth objective. For deeper analysis, AMC can cross-tab NTB rate against audience segments and creative to surface which targeting approaches are actually generating new buyers.

audit_checks.title: Prospecting campaigns are optimized toward total purchases rather than new-to-brand purchases
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A prospecting campaign goal set to total purchases will direct spend toward the path of least resistance, which is reaching existing customers who are already likely to convert. The campaign spends its budget on audiences that inflate the purchase count while contributing little to brand growth.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP allows advertisers to set campaign goals that guide how the system evaluates performance. For a campaign where the stated objective is customer acquisition, the goal should reference new-to-brand (NTB) purchases, not total purchases. Setting the goal to total purchases sends a different signal: it treats a sale to an existing customer as equally valuable as a sale to a first-time buyer, even though the business objective is specifically to grow the customer base.

The practical effect is that a prospecting campaign with a total-purchase goal can spend its budget on retargeting-adjacent audiences and existing customers while appearing to succeed by the metric it is being measured on. The purchase count looks healthy. The NTB rate is not surfaced, so the acquisition gap stays invisible until someone looks at the NTB column.

Amazon DSP supports NTB-specific goal-setting at the order level. You may want to confirm that any order with a customer acquisition objective references NTB purchases as the primary performance goal, and that the goal is reflected in how line items are paced and evaluated.

A few signs point to this misalignment:
  • The campaign brief or media plan describes a prospecting objective, but the Amazon DSP order goal is set to total purchases or ROAS
  • Line items targeting new audiences are evaluated in post-campaign reporting on total purchase volume with no NTB benchmark
  • The NTB rate for the campaign falls below 50% but no adjustment to targeting or goal has been made
You may want to review the goal setting on any order with a prospecting label alongside its NTB rate to confirm the two are aligned.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, the distinction between a prospecting order and a retargeting order should be reflected in the goal setting. A prospecting order targeting new-to-Amazon audiences or product detail page viewers who have not purchased should carry an NTB purchase goal. Setting both order types to total purchases blurs that boundary and makes it harder to evaluate each on its actual purpose.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Non-endemic advertisers do not have access to Amazon NTB purchase data and cannot set NTB purchase goals. This check applies to endemic advertisers only.

investigate.intro: Confirm that orders with a customer acquisition objective are using an NTB purchase goal rather than a total purchase goal, and review the NTB rate against any benchmark set at campaign planning.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and navigate to the order you want to review.
Check the order goal setting. Note whether the goal references NTB purchases or total purchases.
For orders with a stated prospecting objective, compare the goal setting to the campaign brief or the objective documented at campaign launch.
Pull the NTB rate for the order over the campaign flight. Note whether the NTB rate has been reviewed as a performance signal or whether reporting has focused only on total purchases and ROAS.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: In AMC, run a query that shows NTB rate by order and by audience segment to see whether prospecting line items are reaching net-new buyers.
Compare NTB rate across orders with different goal settings to assess whether the goal setting is influencing which audiences the campaign reaches.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per order with a prospecting objective, the goal setting and the NTB rate over the campaign flight. An order with an NTB purchase goal and an NTB rate above the account or category benchmark passes. An order with a total purchase goal on a prospecting campaign, or an NTB rate that has not been reviewed against a benchmark, is a fail recorded with the order name and the goal setting observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the goal setting and NTB rate for each prospecting order above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s prospecting campaigns in Amazon DSP are set to optimize toward total purchases rather than new-to-brand (NTB) purchases. A total purchase goal treats sales to existing customers as equally valuable as sales to first-time buyers, which directs spend toward the path of least resistance rather than toward customer acquisition. {{voice_recommend}} updating the order goal on prospecting campaigns to reference NTB purchases as the primary performance metric, and establishing an NTB rate benchmark against which each prospecting flight can be evaluated. AMC can support that baseline analysis if one is not already in place.

audit_checks.title: New-to-brand and retargeting conversions are reported together without separation
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Combining NTB and retargeting conversions in a single view makes both look better than they are. The NTB rate appears higher because retargeting purchases inflate the total, and the retargeting line items appear to contribute to acquisition when they do not.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP campaigns typically include both prospecting line items, which target audiences who have not recently purchased from the brand, and retargeting line items, which target past visitors or purchasers. The two serve different objectives: prospecting drives customer acquisition, and retargeting drives repeat purchase or cart recovery. New-to-brand (NTB) metrics are meaningful for prospecting and largely irrelevant for retargeting because retargeting by design reaches existing or recent customers.

When NTB and retargeting conversions are reported together, the combined view is misleading in two directions. Retargeting purchases reduce the NTB rate for what is presented as the campaign total, making acquisition appear less efficient than the prospecting lines actually are. At the same time, any NTB purchases that retargeting happens to drive (for example, from a lapsed buyer outside the 12-month window) can be credited to retargeting in a way that makes acquisition look like a secondary benefit of the retargeting budget.

The practical fix is to review NTB rate separately for prospecting and retargeting orders, rather than at an aggregate campaign level. Amazon DSP supports this at the order and line item level in the console. AMC supports it with more granularity.

A few signs point to this issue:
  • Campaign performance reports show a single NTB rate or NTB purchase count for the campaign without breaking it out by order type
  • Prospecting and retargeting orders are grouped under one performance summary in reports delivered to stakeholders
  • The reported NTB rate is used to justify prospecting spend but includes purchases from retargeting line items in the denominator
You may want to confirm that NTB metrics are reviewed separately for prospecting and retargeting orders before being reported to stakeholders.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers running both prospecting and retargeting on Amazon DSP, the order structure directly affects how NTB can be separated. If prospecting and retargeting audiences are in the same order, separating the NTB data requires AMC. If they are in separate orders, the console surfaces the separation natively. You may want to confirm that the order structure supports the reporting separation the campaign needs.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: Non-endemic advertisers do not have access to Amazon NTB purchase data. This check applies to endemic advertisers only.

investigate.intro: Confirm that NTB purchase data is being reviewed separately for prospecting and retargeting orders rather than combined into a single campaign-level metric.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and pull NTB purchases and NTB rate at the order level, broken out by individual order rather than at the campaign total.
Identify which orders are prospecting and which are retargeting. Compare the NTB rate for prospecting orders to the NTB rate for retargeting orders separately.
If only a single combined NTB figure is available at the campaign level, note that prospecting and retargeting may be in the same order, which limits the separation available from the console alone.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: In AMC, run a query that segments NTB purchases by line item and by audience type to separate the contribution of prospecting audiences from retargeting audiences.
Review whether any NTB purchases are being attributed to retargeting line items and confirm whether that reflects genuine lapsed buyers or a structural reporting issue.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner confirms whether NTB purchases and NTB rate are available separately for prospecting and retargeting orders, either in the console or through AMC. Campaigns where the NTB rate is reviewed separately per order type pass. Campaigns where NTB is reported only at a combined campaign level, or where prospecting and retargeting orders share a single NTB figure without any separation, are a fail recorded with the campaign name and the reporting structure observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the NTB reporting structure for prospecting and retargeting orders above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP campaigns report new-to-brand (NTB) conversions at a combined campaign level without separating prospecting and retargeting orders. Combining the two distorts the NTB rate in both directions: retargeting purchases dilute what prospecting has actually contributed to acquisition, while any NTB purchases from retargeting are credited in a way that overstates acquisition efficiency. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing NTB purchases and NTB rate separately for prospecting and retargeting orders, either through the order-level view in the Amazon DSP console or through an AMC query that segments by audience type. Reporting the two separately gives a cleaner picture of what each part of the campaign is actually doing.
